Physical pendulum is rigid body oscillating about fixed axis.
Total SHM energy equals kinetic energy plus potential energy.
SHM displacement follows sinusoidal equations; extrema and zeros characterize motion behavior.
Period relates to angular frequency; spring and pendulum periods follow specific formulas.
SHM is a specific type of periodic motion with special characteristics.
Scalars have magnitude; vectors have magnitude and direction.
Forces are vectors describing interactions; always result from object interactions.
Central object motion is negligible when satellite mass is much smaller.
Total kinetic energy equals translational plus rotational kinetic energy.
Total angular momentum equals sum of all constituent parts' momenta.
Objects treated as point particles with mass and charge properties.
Net force equals the vector sum of all forces on a system.
Angular momentum of rigid systems equals moment of inertia times angular velocity.
Torque transfers energy when applied through angular displacement.
Net torque causes angular velocity to change in rotating systems.
Rotational and translational equilibrium are independent; net torque zero maintains constant angular velocity.
Rotational inertia measures resistance to rotation based on mass distribution.
Motion can be represented through multiple formats and representations.
Newton's third law explains interactions through paired forces between objects.
Unbalanced forces produce nonzero net force on a system.
Torque depends only on the perpendicular force component to the position vector.
Linear distance traveled equals radius times angular displacement.
Angular displacement measures rotation angle in radians about an axis.
Elastic collisions conserve total kinetic energy of the system.
Reference frame choice determines direction and magnitude of measured quantities.
Gravitational force depends on mass and distance, acts attractively along center-to-center line.
System center-of-mass velocity is constant without net external forces.
Decompose multidimensional motion into one-dimensional kinematic analysis by components.
Kinetic friction acts opposite to relative motion, independent of contact area.
Momentum equals mass times velocity.
Power measures the rate of energy change through transfer or conversion.
System properties emerge from interactions between constituent objects.
Ideal springs exert force proportional to length change from equilibrium.
Single-object systems contain only kinetic energy.
Systems with conservative forces between objects possess potential energy.
Translational kinetic energy equals one-half mass times velocity squared.
Centripetal acceleration points toward circle center; magnitude equals v²/r.
Resistive forces oppose motion and depend linearly on velocity.
Lever arm is the perpendicular distance from axis to force line.
Arrows represent vectors with length proportional to magnitude.
Displacement measures change in position from initial to final location.
Three kinematic equations describe constant acceleration motion in one dimension.
Measurements convert between different reference frames.
Velocity and acceleration differ across dimensions and need not be uniform.
Systems can be modeled as single objects when internal details are irrelevant.
Contact forces result from touching; macroscopic effects of interatomic electric forces.
Internal forces do not affect a system's center of mass motion.
Translational equilibrium occurs when net force on system equals zero.
System acceleration is proportional to net force, in same direction.
Gravitational field represents force per unit mass; acceleration equals field strength numerically.
Kinetic friction equals coefficient times normal force; depends on materials.
Spring force magnitude follows Hooke's law with proportionality constant.
Newton's second law yields differential equations; separation of variables integrates velocity with initial conditions to find position and acceleration.
Various forces provide centripetal acceleration; gravity alone at loop top minimum speed.
Kinetic energy has magnitude only, no directional component.
Potential energy is a scalar quantity dependent on object positions.
Multi-object systems can have kinetic and potential energies.
Average power equals total energy transferred divided by elapsed time.
Momentum is a vector with velocity direction.
Total system momentum equals sum of individual object momenta.
Individual object kinetic energies change; total kinetic energy remains constant.
Angular velocity is the time rate of change of angular position.
Linear and angular kinematics quantities relate through radius multiplier.
Rotational inertia for point mass equals mass times squared distance.
Unbalanced torques cause angular velocity to change in rigid systems.
Angular acceleration is proportional to net torque and inversely proportional to rotational inertia.
Work by torque equals integral of torque over angular displacement.
Angular momentum uses cross product of position and momentum; axis selection and geometry matter.
Angular momentum changes only through external interactions; system selection determines conservation.
Rolling without slipping relates center-of-mass motion to rotational motion.
Circular orbits maintain constant total energy, potential energy, angular momentum, and kinetic energy.
Restoring force proportional to displacement causes SHM; equilibrium is zero net force.
SHM position satisfies the differential equation d²x/dt² = −ω²x from Newton's second law.
Total energy in SHM systems remains constant over time.
Period formula derived from rotational Newton's second law for small amplitudes.
Angular momentum is always conserved in physical interactions.
Total rotational inertia is sum of individual rotational inertias.
Elliptical orbits conserve total energy and angular momentum; potential and kinetic energy vary.
Weight is the gravitational force on an object near an astronomical body.
Systems can exchange energy and mass with their environment.
Mechanical energy equals kinetic plus potential energy.
Tangential acceleration changes speed; directed along the circle's path.
Gravitational force on center of mass creates restoring torque from equilibrium.
Velocity changes only when nonzero net external force acts.
Distance and speed are scalars; position, displacement, velocity, acceleration are vectors.
Zero net force means system velocity remains constant.
Average power equals total work done divided by time interval.
Motion in one dimension is independent of perpendicular dimensional changes.
Tension results from internal forces in strings; properties vary by ideal conditions.
Momentum analyzes collisions with large internal forces and explosions separating objects.
Observed velocity combines object and observer motion via vector addition; acceleration is frame-invariant.
Terminal velocity is maximum speed when constant and resistive forces balance, yielding zero net force.
Internal momentum changes balance; system selection determines if momentum conserves.
Spring force direction always points toward system equilibrium position.
Friction in ideal rolling without slipping dissipates no energy.
SHM characteristics derived from x = A cos(ωt + φ); acceleration a = −ω²x; vmax = Aω, amax = Aω².
Inelastic collisions result in decreased total kinetic energy.
Angular acceleration is the time rate of change of angular velocity.
Free-body diagrams visualize forces and help determine equations for situations.
Kinetic energy values depend on the reference frame of measurement.
Rigid body points share identical angular velocity and acceleration values.
Gravitational acceleration near Earth's surface is constant and downward.
Force diagrams show forces, magnitudes, directions, and locations relative to rotation axis.
Angular impulse equals torque integrated over time interval.
Average quantities depend on initial and final states over time interval.
Torque-angle graph area represents work done by torque.
Rotating systems require independent linear and rotational motion analysis.
Maximum kinetic energy occurs when potential energy is minimum.
Zero potential energy reference point is observer-chosen for analytical convenience.
Static friction acts between non-moving surfaces in contact.
Solid rotational inertia found by integrating mass elements squared distance.
Individual objects behave differently from each other and the system.
Velocity changes only in direction of unbalanced force components.
Maximum potential energy occurs at minimum kinetic energy; amplitude changes affect total energy.
Projectile motion has zero acceleration one direction, constant acceleration perpendicular.
Static friction prevents sliding up to maximum value; depends on coefficient.
Rotational kinematics equations parallel linear motion with constant acceleration.
Vectors expressed as magnitude-direction or unit vector notation with i, j, k components.
Average velocity equals displacement divided by elapsed time interval.
Instantaneous power is the derivative of work with respect to time.
Net acceleration combines centripetal and tangential acceleration vectors.
Change in potential energy equals negative integral of conservative force over displacement.
Series springs: reciprocal of equivalent constant equals sum of reciprocals.
Instantaneous velocity equals the slope of position-time graph.
Torque equals r cross F; cross-product magnitude is AB sin θ with right-hand rule direction.
Free-body diagrams show all forces exerted on object by environment.
Gravitational force can be treated as constant when change in distance is negligible.
Lost kinetic energy converts to heat, sound, deformation via nonconservative forces.
Gravitational potential energy is zero at infinite separation; Ug = −G(m₁m₂)/r.
Small-angle approximation simplifies restoring torque equation for small oscillations.
Zero net external torque means constant total angular momentum.
Energy changes must balance within system or transfer externally.
Resonance occurs at natural frequency, increasing amplitude when external force matches system frequency.
Conservation of momentum determines velocities before and after collisions.
Angular impulse direction aligns with the applied torque direction.
Slipping decouples center-of-mass motion from rotational motion.
Circular orbit total energy relates to potential and kinetic energy: K = −½U.
Period and frequency describe uniform circular motion; T = 2πr/v.
Conservative force equals negative potential energy slope; points toward decreasing energy.
Systems can be defined with constant total energy.
Instantaneous power equals force times velocity times cosine of angle.
Small-angle approximation yields second-order SHM differential equation.
Momentum conservation applies universally to all interactions.
Perfectly inelastic collisions result in objects moving together with one velocity.
Instantaneous acceleration equals the slope of velocity-time graph.
SHM period is independent of amplitude; changing amplitude does not alter period.
Rotational inertia is minimum when axis passes through center of mass.
Average acceleration equals velocity change divided by time interval.
Angular impulse equals area under torque-versus-time graph.
Opposite directions in 1D are represented by opposite signs.
Internal system structure significantly affects how to analyze systems.
Nonzero net torque transfers angular momentum to environment.
Kinetic friction during slipping dissipates energy from the system.
Inertial frames are where Newton's first law can be verified.
Forces originate from center of mass point; system mass concentrated there.
Earth's surface gravitational field strength approximates 10 N/kg or 10 m/s².
Static friction coefficient typically exceeds kinetic friction coefficient.
Series arrangement yields equivalent constant smaller than any individual.
Graphical representations can determine and analyze SHM properties.
Change in angular momentum is final minus initial angular momentum.
Zero net external force means total system momentum remains constant.
Orbital centripetal acceleration from gravity; period relates to mass via Kepler's law.
Displacement equals the area under the velocity-time curve.
Escape velocity allows satellite to reach infinite distance with zero final speed.
Align axes parallel to acceleration direction to simplify algebraic representation.
Energy change equals net external energy transfer.
Parallel springs: equivalent constant equals sum of individual constants.
Apparent weight equals the normal force magnitude exerted on an object.
Stable equilibrium: small displacement produces restoring force back to equilibrium.
Simple pendulum models point mass at distance from pivot point.
External changes can alter a system's internal structure.
Acceleration occurs when velocity magnitude or direction changes.
Parallel axis theorem relates inertia about offset axis to center of mass.
Energy is conserved universally in all interactions.
Escape velocity achieves zero total mechanical energy; vesc = √(2GM/r).
Small time intervals yield average values approximating instantaneous values.
Change in velocity equals the area under the acceleration-time curve.
Unstable equilibrium: small displacement produces force away from equilibrium.
Angular impulse equals change in angular momentum; follows from Newton's second law.
Torsion pendulum exhibits SHM with restoring torque proportional to displacement.
Nonzero net force transfers momentum between system and environment.
Accelerating systems experience apparent weight different from gravitational force.
Center of mass lies on symmetry lines for symmetric distributions.
Center of mass position calculated from weighted sum of particle positions.
Instantaneous velocity is derivative of position with respect to time.
Zero work and no nonconservative forces conserve mechanical energy.
Stable equilibrium occurs at local minima of potential energy function.
Instantaneous acceleration is derivative of velocity with respect to time.
Nonzero work transfers energy between system and environment.
Center of mass for continuous bodies uses integral of mass density.
Unstable equilibrium occurs at local maxima of potential energy function.
Systems behave as single objects located at their center of mass.
Elastic potential energy of ideal springs described by standard spring equation.
